title: Banks as Liquidity Multipliers
abstract: We characterize the interaction between banks’ liquid assets purchases and deposit issuance decisions. Using global games, we derive aliquidity multiplier: the amount of deposits a bank can create when endowed with one additional unit of liquid asset to maintain a given level of liquidity risk. In our central theorem, we prove it is larger than unity. This entails that banks have a special role in enhancing liquidity provision, “multiplying” liquid assets into a larger quantity of deposits. Our theory has implications for banks’ balance sheet choices, the pricing of liquid securities, and the role of public liquidity provision.

article.1 The Model:
JEL C72 - Noncooperative Games G01 - Financial Crises G21 - Banks; Depository Institutions; Micro Finance Institutions; Mortgages Editor: Itay Goldstein Itay Goldstein Editor Search for other works by this author on: Oxford Academic Google Scholar

A key economic function of banks is to provide liquidity. They do so by producing claims that are useful for transacting, namely, short-term debt, with the leading example being demand deposits. These claims deliver money-like benefits to their holders. However, producing them exposes banks to (funding) liquidity risk, namely, the risk of incurring losses because of an inability to meet short-term debt obligations when such obligations become due. Banks mitigate this risk by holding a liquidity buffer that comprises high-quality liquid assets (HQLAs) “that consists of cash or assets that can be converted into cash at little or no loss of value in private markets” (BCBS 2013). In an analogy to a plant due toStein (2013), a bank uses an asset portfolio as inputs toproducedeposits as the main output, as well as an unwanted by-product: liquidity risk.

In the case of a narrow bank, which by definition must have sufficient liquidity to back all its demand deposits, the “production technology” is trivial: producing one additional unit of deposit requires one additional unit of HQLA as input. In general, however, the interaction between assets and liabilities is more subtle, because banks use a mix of HQLAs and (illiquid) long-term loans as input to produce new (liquid) short-term debt. In so doing, banks can “multiply” a given quantity of HQLAs into a larger quantity of private liquid claims and thereby enhance the provision of liquidity in the economy. It is this role of banks as liquidity multipliers that we seek to evidence and investigate in this paper.

We consider an economy in which households directly derive utility from holdings of liquid claims, which both banks (private liquidity, i.e., demand deposits) and the government (public liquidity, i.e., a risk-free government bond that plays the role of a generic HQLA) create. In our central result we show that, when endowed with one additional unit of HQLA, a bank can createmorethan one unit of new deposits without changing its liquidity risk. This result implies that banks can enhance the provision of liquidity by multiplying the scarce supply of public liquidity into a larger supply of private liquidity. Importantly, this ability to multiply liquidity exists only inasmuch as banks take on liquidity risk: a bank that wishes to completely avoid liquidity risk would need to fully back one additional unit of deposit with one additional unit of HQLA. Thus, in our theory, banks taking on liquidity risk or acting as liquidity multipliers are two sides of the same coin.

Our paper also sheds light on how banks optimally choose their balance sheet composition, how the liquidity premium priced into liquid assets is linked to the balance sheet decisions of banks, and how variations in the available quantity of HQLAs can affect welfare and economywide liquidity risk.

We introduce a liquid asset in a global games model of bank runs with endogenous balance sheet decisions. Our three-dates model features two classes of agents: banks and creditors. Banks hold a risk-free (liquid) government bond and a risky (illiquid) long-term project; they are funded by a mix of deposits and equity. Creditors are willing to pay a price premium for deposits because they enjoy “convenience” benefits from holding liquid assets. This price premium makes deposits a cheap source of funding for banks but exposes them to runs. A run occurs when creditors withdraw their funds because they anticipate that others are going to withdraw and the bank is going to fail. Banks can mitigate this risk by building a portfolio of liquid government bonds that can be sold at no loss to satisfy early withdrawals. It follows that the price of government bonds also contains a premium due to their role in the liquidity risk management of banks.

We model runs as a global game between depositors à laGoldstein and Pauzner (2005). The success probability of the bank’s risky project—the fundamental—is unknown ex ante, but creditors obtain a noisy signal of its value. Given their private signal, each creditor forms beliefs about the value of the fundamental and the actions of other creditors. Using standard global games techniques, we derive the unique equilibrium in threshold strategies of our model: each creditor runs on the bank if the signal they receive is below this threshold. For a range of realizations of the fundamental, a run forces the bank to liquidate its risky project at a loss. The expected value of this loss is how we define liquidity risk. Consistent with intuition, we prove that liquidity risk increases with the amount of demand deposits issued, and decreases with the size of the liquidity buffer.

A bank’s iso-risk curve shows the various combinations of debt level and liquidity buffer that result in the same level of liquidity risk. The slope of the iso-risk curve—to which we attach the symbolμ—measures the quantity of new deposits a bank can create without changing its liquidity risk if it marginally increases its liquidity buffer. In our central theorem, we prove that this slope is larger than unity, so we nameμaliquidity multiplier.

Next, we will characterize the optimal decisions of banks. In this environment a bank manager must make two decisions. What the bank’sliquidity risk appetiteshould be: in which states the bank must be able to survive, and in which states it will fail. And, for a given liquidity risk appetite, what thebalance sheet compositionshould be: on the active side, the size of its liquidity buffer, and on the passive side, the size of its deposits.

Consider first the balance sheet composition for a given liquidity risk target: purchasing one additional unit of HQLA, a bank can produceμmore deposits and capture the benefits priced into them. Now, for any (nonzero) liquidity risk target, the liquidity multiplierμis strictly above one, and approaches one as the quantity of HQLAs and deposits simultaneously increase (in the ratio required to keep the liquidity risk unchanged). These properties have two consequences. First, in equilibrium the liquidity premium of government bonds must exceed that of bank deposit. Otherwise, a bank could always capture higher total net liquidity benefits—the liquidity benefits captured by issuing deposits minus the premium paid to build an HQLA portfolio—by creatingμadditional units of deposits backed by one unit of HQLA. Second, a bank optimally continues to expand its balance sheet in this way until the point where the net liquidity benefits no longer increase, since the multiplier becomes too weak.

Turning to the determination of the liquidity risk appetite, start with the case of anarrow bank, namely, one that holds enough liquidity to repay the entirety of its deposits in case all creditors run on the bank. We prove that such a bank is not exposed to any liquidity risk, and that it must fully back a marginal increase in deposits with an equally sized increase in its liquidity buffer to maintain zero liquidity risk, that is, its liquidity multiplier is one. We then show that—as long as the creditors’ demand for liquid claims is not satiated—following this safe strategy is not optimal for banks, as a small substitution of equity for deposits generates a first-order reduction in the bank’s financing costs and only a negligible increase in liquidity risk.

These results imply that banks use each unit of government bond as input to produce more than one unit of bank deposits, plus some liquidity risk as the unwanted residual of this production process. Bank deposits are therefore an imperfect liquid asset compared to the HQLA asset, as they are not completely risk-free. Nevertheless, the presence of a liquidity multiplier allows banks to enhance the provision of liquidity in the economy. This arrangement is not just privately, but also socially optimal. We show that a social planner whose only prerogative is to set leverage and liquidity requirements, that is, to control the quantity of deposits issued, and HQLAs held, by banks, would do no better than the private solution. We prove, however, that increasing the supply of government bonds (if at no cost) always improves welfare; but it may increase the occurrence of liquidity crises, because in equilibrium banks respond to the lower cost of holding HQLAs by increasing their deposit issuance.

Finally, our model delivers insights into the pricing of liquid assets. We show that the premium that a bank pays, in the form of a low yield, to hold HQLAs, connects both to an intrinsic property of the ultimate consumers of liquidity —the investors’ marginal benefit of holding liquid assets—and to an intrinsic property of those who produce it—the banks’ liquidity multiplier. This is because the value of an HQLA lies in that it is an input that enables banks to produce more deposits that are valuable to creditors; how much more is measured by the liquidity multiplier, how valuable depends on the investors’ marginal valuation of liquidity. Since the liquidity multiplier is strictly above one, holding one additional unit of liquid assets enables a bank to harvest the money premium priced into depositson more than one unit of notional. This ability to earn a “levered” money premium on their liquid asset holdings makes banks the natural buyers of government bonds, as banks bid up their price above the marginal valuation of creditors. It also means that in our equilibrium, banks own the entire supply of HQLAs, even though creditors are the ones with an intrinsic preference for liquid claims.

Our paper connects with several strands in banking economics. First, we contribute to the literature emphasizing the interactions between the asset and liability sides of bank balance sheets. We follow the perspective ofGorton and Pennacchi (1990),Gorton (2010),DeAngelo and Stulz (2015), orHanson et al. (2015), all of whom view banks as producers of safe/liquid liabilities taking other safe/liquid claims as an input on their asset side.Gennaioli, Shleifer, and Vishny (2013)show how (shadow) banks can increase their borrowing capacity by resorting to securitization to reduce asset-side risk. Our model equilibrium echoes theirs in one aspect: their investors are infinitely risk-averse, yet it is banks who hold the perfectly safe asset.Drechsler, Savov, and Schnabl (2018)develop a model in which monetary policy influences the cost incurred by banks to hold liquid assets and thus, indirectly, the cost of short-term leverage.Kashyap, Rajan, and Stein (2002)show that bank lending (via lines of credit) and deposit taking are synergistic activities as soon as deposit withdrawals and lines of credit drawdowns are imperfectly correlated.Holmström and Tirole (1998)analyze the liquidity demand of credit-constrained firms. Their firms, like our banks, can avoid liquidations of positive net present value projects by holding government bonds, which can be sold to raise funds. When liquidity is scarce, government bonds sell at a premium in equilibrium and increasing their supply can improve welfare.Pennacchi (2012)provides a historical review of narrow banks and discusses the theoretical and quantitative evidence in favor of narrow banking policies. InWeymuller (2015), banks act as “safety multipliers,” by using public debt as an input to issue safe liabilities to risk-averse investors. In his theory, banks take advantage of the hedging benefits of government bonds to reduce asset side risk, whereas our focus is on their liquidity properties and role as a run mitigant.

Second, we are indebted to the literature developing and applying global games theory in banking economics, notablyRochet and Vives (2004),Goldstein and Pauzner (2005), andMorris and Shin (2006,2009). This literature shows how global games theory can be used to capture formally a coordination failure among debt holders and resolve the multiplicity of equilibria present in the classic paper ofDiamond and Dybvig (1983).Liu and Mello (2011)study the asset side liquidity risk management strategies of hedge funds.Liu (2016)models the interactions between creditor runs and the supply of, and demand for, liquidity in the interbank market.Liu (2023)extends his ideas to a dynamic setting. In terms of modeling, the paper closest to ours isCarletti, Goldstein, and Leonello (2020). They analyze the balance sheet decisions of banks in a model featuring endogenous runs in the traditionGoldstein and Pauzner (2005)and fire-sale externalities. They derive key insights on the specific roles of Basel-III-type capital and liquidity requirements.1Our goal is different: by introducing a liquid asset, we evidence the mechanism of liquidity multiplication and study how the premium commanded by liquid assets depends on the balance sheet decisions of banks.

In non-global-games, continuous-time settings,Tourre (2016)shows that liquid assets are also effective at preventing runs driven by an intertemporal coordination failure, andHugonnier and Morellec (2017)study the impact of liquidity and leverage requirements on banks’ financing decisions and insolvency risk.

Finally, our paper also connects to the literature studying the convenience yield priced into near-money assets, such as U.S. Treasuries:Krishnamurthy and Vissing-Jorgensen (2012),Greenwood, Hanson, and Stein (2015)andNagel (2016).2Representative agent models are used to motivate their empirical analysis. Our model points to the liquidity risk management strategy of banks as being a key driver of this convenience yield.
There are three dates (t= 0, 1, 2), a single, divisible good, and two classes of agents in the economy: banks and creditors, who do not discount the future.

2.1 Characterization of creditors’ run decisions

2.1.1 Equilibrium of the run/stay game

Proposition 1 (Equilibrium in Threshold Strategies). In the limit of vanishing noise, the global game defined above has a unique equilibrium in threshold strategies. For realizations of p above p * ⁠ , all creditors remain invested and the bank survives. For realizations of p below p * ⁠ , all creditors withdraw early and the bank fails.

Under certain conditions, it can be shown that an equilibrium in threshold strategies, such as the one described in the previous section, is the unique Bayesian Nash Equilibrium of such symmetric, binary action global games (Morris and Shin, 2006). One key condition is that the game featuresglobal strategic complementaritiesbetween players’ actions, that is, that the payoff gainv(p,n) is nondecreasing inn.13This property, however, is not satisfied in our model: asFigure 2illustrates, for certain values ofpthe functionv(p,·)features two segments where it isincreasing inn.14Nevertheless, the functionn↦v(p,n)still features the single-crossing property which, as we know fromGoldstein and Pauzner (2005)andMorris and Shin (2006), under the model assumptions guarantees existence and uniqueness of a monotonic pure strategy equilibrium.

Narrow Banks: The Case in which the Multiplier is One

Theorem 1 says that banks can use a one-unit increase in their liquidity buffer to expand their debt by more than one unit without affecting their liquidity risk. Importantly, this ability to “multiply liquidity” goes hand in hand with beingexposedto liquidity risk. Indeed, we saw earlier that a bank can completely eliminate the risk of inefficient liquidations by setting its liquidity ratio equal to one, by being a “narrow bank.” Such a bank cannot act as liquidity multiplier. The reason is that, to keep its liquidity risk nil, the bank must maintain its liquidity ratio at 100%, which requires compensating any incremental debt issuance by an equal-sized increase of its liquid assets buffer.

2.3 General equilibrium

2.3.1 Equilibrium characterization

Since banks can earn a “levered” liquidity premium—by issuing debt equal to a multiple of their liquid asset holdings—they are willing to pay a higher price for government bonds than the valuation of creditors. Competition among banks for the limited supply of government bonds compels them to do so. Despite being the ones with an intrinsic valuation for liquidity, creditors are outbidden by bankers, who are the “natural buyers” of government bonds in our model and hold the entire stock in equilibrium,ββ(pT)=B.Government bonds trade atqb=1+Sbin the initial period, whereSbfollows from the first-order condition of the bank’s portfolio choice,Equation (30). Finally, banks issueδ^(B,pT)>ℓ+Bof debt, where the run thresholdpTsatisfies(35).

The next Proposition summarizes these results.

Proposition 4. In equilibrium: Banks hold the entire stock of government bonds: β β = B ⁠ . Banks take on exposure to the risk of inefficient liquidations, p T > p E ⁠ , where p T satisfies (35) with ∂ δ ^ / ∂ p T being evaluated at ( B , p T ) ⁠ . The bank debt and government bond money premiums are strictly positive and given by (27) and (33) , respectively.

2.3.2 Asset liquidity premiums

Because banks are the marginal investors in government bonds, in general equilibrium, the first-order optimality condition of their portfolio choice becomes a pricing equation for government bonds. Using the formulas established forSbandSd, we see that one implication of our theory is that the government bond and bank debt money premiums satisfy the ranking:

S b > Ψ ′ ( L ) > S d . (36)

The spreadSb−Ψ′(L)reflects the ability of banks to act as liquidity multipliers. The spreadΨ′(L)−Sdreflects the penalty for the bank’s liquidity risk.

The ranking in(36)implies that acquiring one more unit of government bond funded by issuing one more unit bank debt implies a P&L ofSd−Sb<0⁠, which means that this operation is a “negative carry” trade. This observation is consistent with the data:Hoerova et al. (2018)compare the average interest rate on total deposits of households and nonfinancial corporations in euro area banks to the average yield on 1-year sovereign bonds of nonstressed euro area countries, which they use as a proxy for the yield on (level 1) HQLA assets. They evidence that the sovereign bond yield declined—and then remained—below the deposit rate in early 2008. The explanation our model provides is that banks have the ability to capture the premium paid by creditors for liquid assets on a notional equal to amultipleof their liquidity buffer. Banks can still profitably purchase government bonds as an input for the production of liquid assets that are valuable to investors.

2.3.3 Welfare

Because of the coordination failure between creditors, runs precipitate in some cases an inefficient liquidation of the bank. But it is important to distinguish between inefficiency of ex post run decisions by creditors (which is due to the information structure), on the one hand, and inefficiency of ex ante balance sheet decisions by banks, on the other. In our model, banks fully internalize the impact of the choice of capital structure and liquidity buffer on the run decisions of creditors. Indeed, we now show that the equilibrium is constrained-optimal, in the sense that a social planner whose only tools are a liquidity ratio requirement and a HQLA-to-deposit ratio requirement would not seek to alter the banks’ private choices.

Definition 2 (Welfare). We define welfare by treating banks and creditors symmetrically: ω W ≡ U + V − ∑ t = 0 2 ω t , (37) where V is the banks’ initial equity value, U is the creditors’ expected utility, and ω t is the creditors’ endowment in period t . 19

Note that our measure of welfare also corresponds to the sum of expected output and the liquidity benefits to its creditors:

W = V E − ∫ p E p * ( p z − ℓ ) d H ( p ) + Ψ ( L ) . (38)

Asocially optimalallocation is a choice ofβδ(β,b,δ)maximizingWunder the constraints that the quantity of bonds allocated to creditors,b, is nonnegative and that the quantity of bonds allocated to banks,β, is nonnegative and satisfiesββ+b=B⁠.20

Proposition 5 (Constrained efficiency). The socially optimal allocation is privately optimal.

This property of the equilibrium has two implications. First, it is (privately and socially) optimal for banks to intermediate holdings of government bonds by using these bonds as input for the production of private money-like assets. Put simply, government bonds are more valuable sitting on banks’ balance sheets than held in the creditors’ portfolios. Second, a coordination failure is not sufficient per se to justify regulations aiming at curtailing the leverage of banks and/or imposing a minimum liquidity buffer. Rather, it is the interaction of the coordination failure with distortions affecting the banks’ choices that can justify a policy intervention. As we show in the proof of Proposition 5—in addition to the liquidity multiplier being above one—the constrained-efficiency property of our equilibrium also relies on the fact that the liquidation value𝓁lis exogenous: our model does not feature fire-sale externalities. For a global games model where fire-sale externalities create a role for liquidity regulation, seeCarletti, Goldstein, and Leonello (2020).

There is, however, a clear scope to improve welfare by increasing the supply of liquid assets that can be held by the banking sector, as discussed in the next section. The provision of liquidity can also take place ex post in the form of emergency liquidity assistance by a lender of last resort. We briefly return to this point in our concluding remarks.

2.3.4 Public liquidity provision

So far, we have considered the supply of government bonds,B, as fixed. We now discuss how the equilibrium changes whenBvaries. We first show that, when public liquidity creation is costless, it is socially optimal to issue as much of it as possible.

Proposition 6. If the issuance of government bonds does not incur any costs, then welfare is increasing in B .

Proposition 6 is consistent with intuition. Since banks hold a larger liquidity buffer, they are able to issue a larger amount of demandable debt with no significant impact on their liquidity risk, which increases the aggregate liquidity benefits enjoyed by creditors,Ψ(L)⁠.

Note, however, that the larger liquidity buffer held by banks does not necessarily imply a lower run probability in the new equilibrium, because banks respond to the lower cost of holding low-yielding liquid assets (lowerSb) by relying more on the comparatively cheaper source of financing that is short-term debt. InFigure 4, panel 4a illustrates that the run thresholdp*can be nonmonotonic inB. (The considered example usesΨ(L)=L1/2⁠.)

We can get further intuition on the role of banks as liquidity multipliers and its link to the public supply of HQLAs by looking at the following case. Suppose that instead of being everywhere increasing, the liquidity benefits functionΨof creditors satiates at some point, sayL¯⁠. Then, if the government bond supplyBis sufficiently large, creditors can dispense of the banking sector, by directly purchasing bonds to reach their desired level of benefitsL¯⁠. Banks no longer have an incentive to get exposure to liquidity risk, and decide to be narrow: excessive debt issuance can trigger inefficient liquidations, but is no longer remunerated by creditors. In this situation, the liquidity multiplier equals one and the interaction between banks’ asset and liability side choices becomes trivial. Banks are indifferent between letting creditors purchase the HQLAs, or buying some and reissuing thesameamount of private liquidity. Being narrow, the banks issue debt which has no risk penalty, and therefore creditors are indifferent between public and private liquidity.21
article.3 Conclusion:
We have developed a theory characterizing the process by which banks use liquid assets as an input to output demand deposits, and, as an undesirable by-product, some liquidity risk. By making use of global games techniques, we evidenced a key object to understand the interaction between banks’ asset and liability sides choices, which we called a liquidity multiplier. We proved that this multiplier is larger than unity when the degree of liquidity risk is positive. This result is at the root of our theory. Notably, it implies that banks enhance liquidity provision to the economy by performing liquidity multiplication: take as an input some amount of public liquidity and produce as an output a larger amount of private liquidity. It also has consequences for the pricing of private and public liquid securities and in particular sheds one light on the fact that banks hold large liquidity buffers even though the assets constituting them often have a lower return than banks’ cost of deposit funding. Finally, we discussed the role of public liquidity provision. When increasing the supply of safe, liquid assets is costless, this is always beneficial for welfare. However, banks may not be better off and the economy’s exposure to liquidity risk might increase.

We would like to mention some important avenues for future research. The first one is to introduce emergency liquidity assistance in the framework. It is possible to incorporate a lender of last resort (LLR) in our model, while retaining existence, uniqueness, and tractability of equilibrium. Liquidity injections by an LLR are desirable ex post in bad times, but distort the banks’ incentives to manage liquidity risk, which can imply that an LLR facility must be coupled with ex ante regulation (of capital and liquidity). Second, it could be interesting to study the effects of adding heterogeneity across banks at the interim date, as inLiu (2016). Third, investigating the impact of imperfect competition among banks could also deliver new and interesting results.
